text: This is a case report of a robotic-assisted Roux-en-Y cystojejunostomy for a large pancreatic pseudocyst. This procedure was performed at Sinai Grace Hospital, part of the Detroit Medical Center. Our patient is a fifty-six-year-old male with chronic recurrent pancreatitis due to excessive alcohol use. He denied any previous surgeries. Cystic changes were noted on CAT scans as far back as April, evolving and enlarging through July without improvement. His symptoms included epigastric abdominal pain, left upper quadrant pain, nausea, vomiting, and early satiety. During his hospital course, a right upper quadrant ultrasound was completed to rule out gallstones without any cholelithiasis noted. This CT demonstrates the pseudocyst, noted to be twelve point five by seven by eleven centimeters. and compressed between the left lobe of the liver and the stomach, causing a mass effect and displacement of the stomach itself. Preoperatively, our patient was dependent on TPN due to inability to tolerate oral diet. He was sent to subacute rehab for roughly six weeks to allow the cyst to mature and to optimize his nutritional status prior to surgery.

The patient is positioned in the French position in reverse Trendelenburg for a diagnostic laparoscopy. The abdomen is evaluated, revealing the cyst located along the hepatogastric ligament and the lesser curve of the stomach. The robotic XI platform is docked, and port placement is demonstrated with orange indicating eight millimeter trocars and blue indicating twelve millimeter trocars.

The robotic procedure begins with the surgeon running the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz for 40 centimeters. A Maryland bipolar is used to create a small window in the mesenteric border. The small bowel is transected with a 45mm blue load robotic stapler. The small bowel mesentery is incised using a vessel sealer towards the base for mobilization of the Roux limb. The Roux limb is brought anterior to the transverse colon, positioned anticholic and antigastric, and sutured to the most dependent portion of the cyst with a 3-0 barbed suture in a running fashion.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound using a laparoscopic probe through the assistant port, visualizing the cyst with septations. The Roux limb is measured to sixty centimeters and aligned with the biliopancreatic limb without twisting the mesentery. The limbs are sutured together in a seromuscular fashion using silk suture. An enterotomy is created on both the Roux and BP limbs, and a 45mm endo-GIA stapler is used to form an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis. The common enterotomy is closed with a 3-0 running barbed suture in two layers.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to incise the cyst, evacuating chocolate-colored fluid. Due to septations, the cyst is entered for necrosectomy and debridement of its contents. The pseudocyst cavity is shown, followed by copious irrigation with saline and suctioning. The jejunal aneurotomy is formed at the anti-mesenteric border using monopolar scissors.

The surgeon creates the cystojejunostomy using a running three-oh barbed suture. The intraoperative bowel perfusion assessment is performed on the Roux limb and the common channel using near-infrared mode, confirming good blood flow. A fifteen French drain is placed to complete the procedure.

Post-operatively, our patient reported immediate relief of his chronic abdominal pain and his appetite returned almost immediately. He was initiated on a clear liquid diet on post-operative day one and was advanced to a soft diet on post-operative day two, discontinuing his TPN. He was ambulating, urinating, and having bowel function upon discharge on post-operative day two. His JP amylase was negative times two and the JP was removed prior to discharge.

While laparoscopic Roux-en-Y cystojejunostomies are reported in the literature, as shown here with successful outcomes, the use of robotic surgery for this indication is not extensively reported. Our case demonstrates the safe and effective use of robotic surgery for this indication, which allows enhanced articulation for the hand zone anastomosis, better three-D visualization, and increased magnification.
